ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Manchurian; closest_parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03302050) as paneer is the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Paneer Manchurian; A0CKP (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Paneer Manchurian or a sufficiently close parent dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Paneer Manchurian is approximately 250 g, comprising battered and fried paneer cubes tossed in Indo-Chinese Manchurian sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Manchurian — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN component data: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese) and Indo-Chinese sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition), Hyderabad, India; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central for sauce ingredients
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Paneer Manchurian as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Table of Food Composition (2017) for paneer (per 100 g: energy 265 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 20.8 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g) combined with proportional contributions from batter (cornflour), frying oil absorption, and Manchurian sauce ingredients (soy sauce, ketchup, chilli sauce, garlic, ginger, capsicum). Final composite per-100 g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with approximately 50% paneer by weight, 15% batter and oil, and 35% sauce and vegetables. Energy cross-checked against comparable Indo-Chinese paneer dish entries in peer-reviewed Indian dietary studies.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous mixed paneer and starch-sauce dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entries for cheese-based and battered fried dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Manchurian. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous mixed dishes: paneer itself has a very low GI (~15–27 per University of Sydney data for cheese/dairy), while the cornflour batter and sauce contribute moderate glycemic load. The composite GI of approximately 52 reflects the blended contribution of low-GI paneer (~50% by weight) and moderate-GI batter/sauce components (~50% by weight). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 33.0) / 100 = 17.2, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 13.2 × 2.5 = 33.0 g. gi_category set to 'low' as GI 52 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per serving) from soy sauce, chilli sauce, and ketchup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Paneer Manchurian to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium sauce preparations, as a single serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~17.2) driven by cornflour batter and sweet-savoury sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Paneer Manchurian in controlled portions (approximately 150 g), pairing it with high-fibre vegetables or salad to blunt postprandial glucose response, while monitoring total carbohydrate intake from the batter and sauce components.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer (~5.6 g per 100 g) and deep-frying oil, combined with high sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Paneer Manchurian due to its saturated fat and sodium content; opting for a baked or air-fried preparation with reduced sauce can lower saturated fat by approximately 30% and sodium by 20–25%.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish originating in India, widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are coated in a seasoned cornflour-egg batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then tossed in a bold Manchurian sauce made from soy sauce, tomato ketchup, chilli sauce, vinegar, garlic, ginger, green chillies, and capsicum. The dish is served either dry (as a starter or snack) or with a thicker gravy (as a main course accompaniment to fried rice or noodles). The texture contrasts crispy fried paneer exterior with a soft, milky interior, coated in a tangy, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. Nutritionally, Paneer Manchurian is a moderate-energy dish (~198 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from paneer (~9.5 g per 100 g) and significant fat from frying (~12.8 g per 100 g, of which ~5.6 g is saturated). The sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and condiments, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard serving (~250 g) is moderate at ~17.2, relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Paneer provides calcium (~190 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12, making it nutritionally superior to many meat-free protein sources. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan or dairy-free.

Can I eat Paneer Manchurian if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Paneer Manchurian as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN paneer composition data combined with proportional sauce and batter ingredient contributions. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable dairy and starch-sauce dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the primary ingredient (paneer) has well-documented ICMR-NIN values.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) paneer data and USDA FoodData Central sauce ingredient data; no single composite entry exists in any approved primary source for Paneer Manchurian. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for paneer or the sauce components. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Manchurian was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 5. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from frying oil composition (standard vegetable oil) and paneer fat profile per ICMR-NIN. 6. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as paneer is the primary protein source and functions as a meat alternative. 7. Sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce and condiments; restaurant preparations may vary significantly. 8. The Chinese local names (曼彻里安奶酪 / 曼徹里安奶酪) are transliterations used in Singapore Chinese-language menus; no standardised Chinese culinary term exists for this dish. 9. Halal status is assumed based on paneer being dairy-derived and no pork/alcohol ingredients in standard preparation; individual restaurant sourcing should be verified.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN paneer data with systematic proportional estimation for composite dish components.
Estimated GI at 52 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous data for dairy and starch-sauce dishes; documented estimation basis fully.
Added Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean cuisine G6 multilingual requirement.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency: all language-specific alias strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI 52 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions.
